sections.Equation of motion: The respiratory system's pressure-volume behaviour combines lung and chest wall compliance curves; at FRC, the lung's inward elastic recoil and the chest wall's outward recoil are equal and opposite, so the combined respiratory-system pressure is zero. Near TLC the combined curve flattens - disproportionately large pressure increases are needed for small further volume gains, risking over-distension. The equation of motion states that Pmus (muscle pressure) at time t is dissipated to overcome resistive pressure (Pres = Rrs x flow) and elastic pressure (Pel = Ers x volume above FRC). During mechanical ventilation, ventilator pressure (Paw) adds to Pmus, so total pressure PTOT = Pmus + Paw; during fully passive ventilation, Pmus = 0 and PTOT = Paw. Resistance and elastance are not constant through a breath and show considerable flow/volume dependency, especially in lung or chest wall disease (e.g. bronchospasm raises resistance; fibrosis raises elastance).
sections.PCO2 elimination: PaCO2 depends on the balance between CO2 production (VCO2) and alveolar ventilation (V'A): PaCO2 = 0.86 x VCO2 / V'A. Minute ventilation (VE) = tidal volume (VT) x respiratory rate (RR). Alveolar ventilation excludes dead space ventilation (VD), so V'A depends on VT, RR, and the VD/VT ratio; a low-VT/high-RR pattern gives a higher VD/VT (worse CO2 clearance) than a high-VT/low-RR pattern at the same VE, and VT changes affect alveolar ventilation more than RR changes. The 'metabolic hyperbola' (PaCO2 vs VE) shifts markedly with VCO2 (e.g. fever, shivering, sepsis increase it) and VD/VT (e.g. pulmonary embolism or severe hypovolaemia increase it) - at a fixed VE of 12 L/min, PaCO2 can range from about 20 to over 50 mmHg depending on these factors. V/Q mismatch and shunt increase VD/VT. HMEs add 30-50 ml of dead space; switching to a heated humidifier increases V'A without changing VE.
sections.Oxygenation: Mechanisms of hypoxaemia: V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion limitation, alveolar hypoventilation, low FiO2, and decreased mixed venous oxygen content (in shock). In mechanically ventilated ICU patients, only V/Q mismatch and shunt are typically clinically relevant - hypoventilation is prevented by the ventilator, diffusion limitation matters mainly in advanced fibrosis, and low FiO2 is not clinically present. Hypoxaemia is corrected by increasing FiO2 and applying PEEP, but because V/Q mismatch and shunt (not hypoventilation or low FiO2) usually cause ICU hypoxaemia, raising FiO2 alone is often insufficient - which is why mechanical ventilation with PEEP is frequently required.
sections.Positive End Expiratory Pressure: PEEP reopens and stabilises alveoli collapsed by atelectasis or oedema, redistributing extravascular lung water. Beneficial effects: improved oxygenation (increased FRC, better V/Q match, reduced shunt); reduced work of breathing (shifting tidal breathing to a more compliant part of the pressure-volume curve, and in airflow obstruction, substituting for the pressure needed to overcome intrinsic PEEP before triggering); prevention of VILI (less cyclic alveolar opening/closing); improved left ventricular function in preload-independent states (reduced LV transmural pressure/afterload). Detrimental effects: overdistension of normally aerated alveoli (increased dead space, worse CO2 clearance, VILI/barotrauma risk); increased right ventricular afterload; impaired venous return (via increased intrathoracic pressure), which reduces cardiac output when output is preload-dependent. No specific PEEP range can be pre-defined as optimal for a given patient at a given time.
sections.Basic functions of positive pressure ven: ICU ventilators need a pressurised gas source/air-oxygen blender, inspiratory/expiratory valves and circuit, and controllers for valve timing plus monitoring/alarms. Three key variables define a breath: the triggering variable (signal starting inspiration - commonly time, pressure or flow; pressure/flow triggering require the patient to alter circuit pressure/flow to a preset level, and during pressure-triggering the respiratory muscles work isometrically before flow begins); the control variable (what determines pressure delivery - typically volume-targeted [preset tidal volume/flow-time profile] or pressure-targeted [preset pressure]; also instantaneous flow/volume in Proportional Assist Ventilation [PAV], or diaphragmatic electrical activity via an oesophageal catheter in Neurally-Adjusted Ventilatory Assist [NAVA]); and the cycling-off variable (signal ending inspiration - time, pressure [Paw rises ~1.5-3 cmH2O above target], or flow [falls to a preset threshold]; PAV cycles off at zero flow, NAVA when diaphragmatic activity drops below a preset level; a safety time-cutoff exists for unusually long inspiration, e.g. >3 sec). A mode is 'control' if both triggering and cycling-off are time-based (operator-determined) and 'assisted' if patient-effort-determined - this is independent of the volume/pressure-targeted classification. Flow-controlled ventilation (FCV) is a newer mode delivering continuous flow in and out of the lungs; set variables are peak inspiratory pressure, inspiratory flow and I:E ratio, while tidal volume and respiratory rate are dependent variables. Routine scheduled ventilator circuit changes are not considered necessary to prevent VAP.

questions.question: Based on the pressure-volume curve of the respiratory system shown, the curve flattens markedly as volume approaches total lung capacity (TLC). What is the clinical significance for mechanical ventilation?
options: A: Progressively larger pressure increases are needed for each further increment in volume, raising the risk of over-distension
B: Progressively smaller pressure increases achieve each further increment in volume, making recruitment efficient near TLC
C: The flattening reflects rising airway resistance rather than the elastic properties of the lung
D: The flattening applies to the chest wall curve but not to the lung or the combined respiratory system
E: The flattening marks the point of optimal compliance, where tidal ventilation is safest
questions.explanation: As the curve flattens near TLC, compliance falls, so ever-larger pressures are needed for small further volume gains - a warning of over-distension and a mechanism of ventilator-induced lung injury. B describes the opposite relationship, C misattributes it to resistance, D wrongly restricts it to the chest wall, and E mistakes the stiff upper curve for a zone of safe, optimal compliance.
questions.topic: PV curve TLC flattening compliance over-distension VILI risk

questions.question: Under passive conditions, the pressure needed to hold the respiratory system at functional residual capacity (FRC) is approximately zero. What best explains this?
options: A: The lung's inward elastic recoil is balanced by the chest wall's outward recoil at this particular volume
B: Neither the lung nor the chest wall generates any elastic recoil once the system sits at FRC
C: The chest wall stays fully relaxed at every lung volume, so the lung's recoil is the only force present
D: FRC is the lung volume at which airway resistance is lowest, and this is what drives the pressure to zero
E: Respiratory system compliance reaches its maximum at FRC, so no pressure is needed to hold that volume
questions.explanation: FRC is defined as the volume where the lung's inward recoil and the chest wall's outward recoil are equal and opposite, giving a net respiratory-system pressure of zero — the forces balance rather than being absent (as if neither structure recoiled at FRC) or one-sided (as if the chest wall stayed fully relaxed at every volume). Attributing this to airway resistance conflates elastic balance with a resistive property, and invoking maximal compliance is plausible-sounding but incorrect — compliance describes the volume-pressure relationship, not why the recoil forces cancel at this point.
questions.topic: FRC zero pressure lung chest wall recoil balance passive

questions.question: A ventilated patient is held at a fixed minute ventilation of 12 L/min. Based on the relationship shown, which change would most plausibly raise this patient's PaCO2 from about 20 mmHg to over 50 mmHg with minute ventilation unchanged?
options: A: A combined rise in CO2 production and dead-space fraction, such as fever alongside a rising dead space from hypovolaemia
B: A fall in the dead-space fraction, for example when improved pulmonary perfusion recovers previously wasted ventilation
C: A rise in tidal volume with a matching fall in respiratory rate, keeping the minute ventilation the same
D: An improvement in cardiac output that more closely matches pulmonary perfusion to the ventilation
E: A switch from volume-controlled to pressure-controlled ventilation at the same set tidal volume and rate
questions.explanation: The graph shows that at a fixed minute ventilation, PaCO2 rises with both CO2 production and dead-space fraction; a simultaneous increase in each shifts the curve rightward. B and D describe changes that would lower dead-space fraction and thus PaCO2, C would if anything reduce dead-space fraction, and E does not change the determinants of alveolar ventilation.
questions.topic: metabolic hyperbola VCO2 VD/VT PaCO2 minute ventilation dead space fever

questions.question: In a mechanically ventilated ICU patient, which of the following is NOT typically a clinically relevant mechanism of hypoxaemia?
options: A: Ventilation-perfusion mismatch
B: Intrapulmonary shunt
C: Alveolar hypoventilation
D: A reduced mixed venous oxygen content in shock
E: Diffusion limitation in advanced pulmonary fibrosis
questions.explanation: Alveolar hypoventilation is prevented by the ventilator itself, so it is not a clinically relevant cause of hypoxaemia in this setting - unlike V/Q mismatch and shunt, which dominate, a low mixed venous oxygen content in shock, and diffusion limitation in advanced fibrosis, all of which can contribute.
questions.topic: hypoxaemia mechanisms ventilated patient negative stem hypoventilation NOT relevant

questions.question: A patient with ARDS and diffuse bilateral atelectasis is started on PEEP. Through which primary mechanism does PEEP improve oxygenation here?
options: A: By reopening and stabilising collapsed alveoli, which raises functional residual capacity and reduces shunt
B: By raising the cardiac output, which increases oxygen delivery to the tissues even without any change in the lungs
C: By increasing alveolar ventilation and thereby improving the clearance of carbon dioxide from the blood
D: By lowering pulmonary vascular resistance and redistributing blood flow toward better-ventilated lung regions
E: By increasing the fraction of the inspired oxygen that actually reaches the alveoli each breath
questions.explanation: PEEP improves oxygenation chiefly by recruiting and stabilising collapsed alveoli, raising FRC, improving V/Q matching and reducing shunt. B misattributes the effect to cardiac output (PEEP often reduces it), C confuses oxygenation with CO2 clearance, D describes an unrelated vascular mechanism, and E conflates PEEP with a rise in FiO2.
questions.topic: PEEP oxygenation mechanism FRC recruitment shunt reduction ARDS

questions.question: A patient with severe COPD and significant intrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP) is triggering the ventilator with visible difficulty. Applying extrinsic PEEP close to the intrinsic PEEP level would be expected to have which effect on the work of breathing?
options: A: Reduce it, by offsetting much of the pressure the muscles must generate to overcome intrinsic PEEP before triggering
B: Increase it, because the added external pressure must now also be overcome by the patient in order to inhale
C: Leave it unchanged, since PEEP acts on oxygenation rather than on the effort needed to trigger a breath
D: Reduce it, but solely because the improved oxygenation lowers the patient's respiratory drive and effort
E: Increase the risk of dynamic hyperinflation while leaving the triggering effort essentially unchanged
questions.explanation: In airflow obstruction with intrinsic PEEP, matched extrinsic PEEP offsets part of the pressure the inspiratory muscles must generate to overcome intrinsic PEEP before flow can begin, reducing the triggering work. B follows the intuition that any added pressure increases work, C and E deny the triggering effect, and D attributes the benefit to the wrong mechanism (oxygenation rather than the intrinsic-PEEP offset).
questions.topic: PEEP intrinsic auto-PEEP work of breathing triggering offset COPD

questions.question: All of the following are recognised beneficial effects of PEEP EXCEPT:
options: A: Improved oxygenation through an increase in functional residual capacity
B: Reduced work of breathing in patients with intrinsic PEEP
C: Prevention of ventilator-induced lung injury by limiting cyclic alveolar opening and closing
D: An increase in physiological dead space from over-distension of normally aerated alveoli
E: Improved left ventricular function through a reduced transmural pressure in preload-independent states
questions.explanation: An increase in dead space from over-distension of normally aerated alveoli is a detrimental effect of PEEP, worsening CO2 clearance and promoting injury - not a benefit. A, B, C and E are all recognised beneficial effects.
questions.topic: PEEP beneficial effects negative stem overdistension dead space detrimental EXCEPT

questions.question: A ventilator is set so that both the start and the end of every breath are determined entirely by preset time intervals, regardless of patient effort, while the delivered pressure profile is preset. How is this mode best classified?
options: A: Control and pressure-targeted, because triggering and cycling-off are both time-based while pressure is the preset variable
B: Assisted and pressure-targeted, since delivering a set pressure rather than a set volume defines an assisted mode
C: Control and volume-targeted, because a fully controlled mode implies a preset tidal volume
D: Assisted and volume-targeted, because time-based triggering still allows the patient to interact
E: A combination of settings that modern ventilators do not permit
questions.explanation: 'Control' versus 'assisted' depends only on whether triggering and cycling are time- or patient-determined; 'volume' versus 'pressure' is a separate axis describing delivery. Here both are time-based (control) and pressure is preset (pressure-targeted). B wrongly ties pressure delivery to an assisted mode, C ties control to volume, and D misreads time-triggering as patient interaction.
questions.section: Basic functions of positive pressure ventilators
questions.topic: control assisted mode volume pressure targeted orthogonal axes classification

questions.question: A patient is placed on a mode in which the delivered pressure is proportional to the electrical activity of the diaphragm, measured via an oesophageal catheter. Which mode is this, and what determines the delivered pressure?
options: A: Neurally-Adjusted Ventilatory Assist (NAVA); pressure proportional to diaphragmatic electrical activity
B: Proportional Assist Ventilation (PAV); pressure proportional to the patient's instantaneous flow and volume
C: Pressure Support Ventilation; a fixed preset pressure applied to each breath
D: Volume-controlled ventilation; pressure following a preset flow-time profile
E: Flow-controlled ventilation; pressure proportional to the expiratory flow
questions.explanation: NAVA delivers pressure proportional to the diaphragm's electrical activity via an oesophageal catheter. B describes PAV, where pressure instead tracks instantaneous flow and volume (the closest near-miss), while C, D and E describe fixed-pressure or preset-profile modes that do not follow diaphragmatic activity.
questions.section: Basic functions of positive pressure ventilators
questions.topic: NAVA PAV diaphragmatic electrical activity proportional assist mode distinction

questions.question: The traces compare pressure-triggering (left) and flow-triggering (right). At the marked trigger point, the pressure-triggering panel shows a dip in airway pressure while the flow-triggering panel shows no such pressure dip at that instant. What best explains this?
options: A: Pressure-triggering senses the effort as a pressure fall, while flow-triggering senses a flow change, so airway pressure stays steadier
B: Flow-triggering requires no patient effort at all, so there is no muscular pressure change to appear on the trace
C: The pressure dip is a sign of significant intrinsic PEEP that happens to be present in this particular patient
D: Flow-triggering actually causes an even larger pressure dip that is simply hidden by the compressed scale of this display
E: The difference reflects a change in delivered tidal volume between the two panels rather than the triggering method
questions.explanation: Pressure-triggering works by sensing a fall in circuit pressure caused by the patient's effort, so a Paw dip appears; flow-triggering senses a change in circuit flow instead, so the trigger shows on the flow trace while airway pressure stays steadier. Both modes require genuine patient effort, which rules out the claim that flow-triggering needs no effort at all, and the traces reflect the triggering mechanism itself rather than intrinsic PEEP or a difference in delivered tidal volume between the two panels.
questions.section: Basic functions of positive pressure ventilators
questions.topic: pressure triggering flow triggering waveform mechanism Paw dip flow change

questions.question: Regarding PEEP:

statements.label: A
statements.text: PEEP can reduce ventilator-induced lung injury by limiting the cyclic opening and closing of alveoli.

statements.label: B
statements.text: Whether PEEP raises or lowers cardiac output depends on whether cardiac output is preload-dependent.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A single optimal PEEP range can be defined that applies broadly across different patients and situations.

statements.label: D
statements.text: PEEP can reduce the work of breathing even in patients without airflow obstruction.

questions.explanation: PEEP's lung-protective effect through limiting cyclic alveolar opening and closing is well established, and its haemodynamic effect on cardiac output genuinely does depend on preload-dependency rather than acting uniformly. The claim that a single optimal PEEP range can be defined broadly across patients is false — no such universal range exists; the correct level depends on the individual patient's respiratory mechanics and haemodynamics at a given time. The statement that PEEP can reduce work of breathing even without airflow obstruction is true but easily doubted: PEEP can lower work of breathing by shifting tidal breathing to a more compliant part of the pressure-volume curve, not only via the intrinsic-PEEP mechanism seen in obstruction.
questions.topic: PEEP optimal range myth VILI cardiac output preload work of breathing compliance

questions.question: Regarding triggering and cycling-off variables:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In Proportional Assist Ventilation (PAV), pressure delivery ends when inspiratory flow falls to zero.

statements.label: B
statements.text: During pressure-triggering, the inspiratory muscles contract isometrically before inspiratory flow begins.

statements.label: C
statements.text: In Neurally-Adjusted Ventilatory Assist (NAVA), cycling-off is governed by a preset flow threshold, as in standard flow-cycling.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Modern ventilators have no safety mechanism to end an abnormally prolonged inspiration.

questions.explanation: PAV's flow-cycling mechanism and the isometric muscle contraction preceding flow onset during pressure-triggering are both accurately described. The claim that NAVA cycles off via a preset flow threshold, as in standard flow-cycling, is false — NAVA instead cycles off when diaphragmatic electrical activity falls by a set percentage from its maximum, a fundamentally different, patient-derived signal. The claim that modern ventilators lack any safety mechanism for an abnormally prolonged inspiration is also false: a safety time-cutoff exists, typically ending inspiration if it becomes excessively long (e.g. beyond ~3 seconds).
questions.section: Basic functions of positive pressure ventilators
questions.topic: PAV NAVA cycling off isometric contraction pressure triggering safety cutoff

questions.question: Regarding the classification of ventilation modes:

statements.label: A
statements.text: A mode with time-based triggering and time-based cycling-off is classified as 'control'.

statements.label: B
statements.text: The volume- versus pressure-targeted label depends on which variable governs the delivered breath, not on how it is triggered or cycled.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A pressure-targeted mode with time-based triggering and cycling-off is a control mode.

statements.label: D
statements.text: A mode classified as 'assisted' cannot also be volume-targeted.

questions.explanation: The definitions of a control mode by its time-based triggering and cycling, the independence of the volume- versus pressure-targeted label from how a breath is triggered or cycled, and the classification of a time-triggered, time-cycled, pressure-targeted mode as control are all accurate. The claim that an assisted mode cannot also be volume-targeted is false: control/assisted and volume/pressure-targeted are independent axes, so an assisted mode can be volume-targeted (e.g. patient-triggered volume-controlled breaths). The common error is to treat the two classifications as linked.
questions.section: Basic functions of positive pressure ventilators
questions.topic: control assisted volume pressure targeted orthogonal axes classification independent

questions.question: Regarding the equation of motion in disease states:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In acute bronchospasm, the resistive pressure component of the equation of motion increases.

statements.label: B
statements.text: In pulmonary fibrosis, the elastic pressure component decreases for a given lung volume.

statements.label: C
statements.text: During fully passive ventilation with no patient effort, Pmus is zero and the total applied pressure equals the ventilator's airway pressure.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Respiratory system resistance and elastance remain constant throughout a breath regardless of lung or chest wall disease.

questions.explanation: The rise in resistive pressure during acute bronchospasm and the equivalence between total applied pressure and the ventilator's airway pressure during fully passive ventilation (where Pmus is zero) are both accurately described. The claim that pulmonary fibrosis decreases the elastic pressure component for a given volume points the wrong way: fibrosis reduces compliance (raises elastance), so the elastic pressure increases, not decreases, for a given volume. The claim that resistance and elastance remain constant throughout a breath regardless of disease is also false — both show considerable flow- and volume-dependency through a breath, especially in disease.
questions.topic: equation of motion bronchospasm fibrosis resistance elastance passive ventilation

questions.question: Regarding flow-controlled ventilation (FCV) and basic ventilator function:

statements.label: A
statements.text: In FCV, the ventilator delivers continuous flow during inspiration and continuous negative flow during expiration.

statements.label: B
statements.text: In FCV, the operator sets peak inspiratory pressure, inspiratory flow, and the I:E ratio.

statements.label: C
statements.text: A positive-pressure ventilator's basic components include a pressurised gas source, inspiratory and expiratory valves, and a controller with monitoring and alarms.

statements.label: D
statements.text: Routine, scheduled changes of the ventilator circuit are recommended to prevent ventilator-associated pneumonia.

questions.explanation: FCV's continuous-flow delivery pattern in both phases, the operator-set parameters of peak inspiratory pressure, inspiratory flow, and I:E ratio (with tidal volume and respiratory rate as the dependent variables), and the basic component list for a positive-pressure ventilator are all accurately described. The claim that routine, scheduled ventilator circuit changes are recommended to prevent VAP is false — such changes are not considered necessary to prevent VAP.
questions.section: Basic functions of positive pressure ventilators
questions.topic: flow-controlled ventilation FCV set variables VAP circuit change ventilator components
